edges.description: Loss of HMGCL function blocks hepatic ketone body generation during catabolic states.
Loss of HMGCL function also blocks leucine degradation, causing upstream toxic organic acid accumulation.
edges.target: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

edges.description: HMGCL deficiency blocks ketone body synthesis, producing absent or inappropriately low ketones during fasting and illness.
Failure to generate ketone bodies during fasting leaves patients dependent on circulating glucose, causing hypoketotic hypoglycemia.
Failed ketone-body production during fasting produces the characteristic nonketotic or hypoketotic hypoglycemia phenotype.
Acute decompensation from ketogenesis failure and organic acid accumulation produces metabolic acidosis.
intermediate_mechanisms: Acute catabolism combines impaired ketone production with organic acid accumulation.

edges.description: Acute metabolic crisis can include lactic acid accumulation.
Tachypnea occurs during metabolic decompensation, consistent with respiratory compensation for acidosis.
Acute energy crisis causes reduced arousal and encephalopathic presentation.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hypoglycemia and metabolic acidosis impair cerebral energy availability during crisis.

edges.description: Vomiting occurs during acute metabolic decompensation episodes.
Cardiac vulnerability may reflect loss of ketone-derived energy substrate during stress.
Severe metabolic decompensation can present atypically with fulminant liver failure.
Hepatic metabolic stress during crises can manifest as elevated transaminases.
Impaired hepatic energy metabolism can be accompanied by hepatocellular lipid accumulation.
intermediate_mechanisms: impaired hepatic ketogenesis and fatty-acid handling

edges.description: Severe decompensation can resemble Reye syndrome with hepatic and encephalopathic features.
intermediate_mechanisms: fasting- or infection-triggered metabolic decompensation with hepatic involvement

edges.description: Hepatic involvement during severe crises can impair coagulation factor synthesis.
intermediate_mechanisms: hepatic synthetic dysfunction during metabolic decompensation

edges.description: Hepatomegaly is reported during acute metabolic crises and hepatic metabolic stress.
Blocked leucine degradation increases leucine-related acyl-CoAs and disrupts hepatic acyl-CoA balance.
edges.source: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

edges.description: Accumulation of 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaric acid drives mitochondrial dysfunction in vulnerable brain regions.
edges.source: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

edges.description: Blocked HMG-CoA cleavage produces the characteristic accumulation of HMG.
edges.source: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

edges.description: Leucine pathway blockade produces elevated urinary 3-methylglutaconic acid.
intermediate_mechanisms: Upstream trans-3-methylglutaconyl-CoA is diverted to 3-methylglutaconic acid.
edges.source: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

edges.description: Leucine catabolic block produces elevated urinary 3-methylglutaric acid.
intermediate_mechanisms: Accumulating leucine catabolism intermediates are converted to urinary organic acids.
edges.source: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

edges.description: Urinary 3-methylglutaric acid is a direct readout of the leucine catabolic block.
edges.source: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

edges.description: Blocked leucine catabolism raises C5-OH acylcarnitine, the newborn screening marker.
intermediate_mechanisms: Upstream leucine-derived acyl-CoA species are conjugated to carnitine.
edges.source: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

edges.description: Leucine metabolite loading in HMGCL-deficient hepatocytes reduces acetyl-CoA levels.
Increased ammonia is the biochemical readout of secondary hyperammonemia caused by impaired urea-cycle activation.
Acetyl-CoA depletion limits N-acetylglutamate-dependent urea-cycle activation, causing secondary hyperammonemia.
Acyl-CoA disequilibrium contributes to hypoglycemia during acute crises.
intermediate_mechanisms: Abnormal hepatic acyl-CoA balance impairs gluconeogenic response to pyruvate.

edges.description: HMG-mediated mitochondrial dysfunction contributes to neurologic injury and developmental vulnerability.
intermediate_mechanisms: HMG disrupts brain mitochondrial bioenergetics, redox homeostasis, and mitochondrial dynamics.

edges.description: Seizures are part of the neurologic sequelae of HMGCLD.
Developmental delay is a neurodevelopmental sequela in a subset of patients.
Speech delay reflects neurodevelopmental involvement.
Hypotonia is reported among neurologic findings in HMGCLD cohorts.
White matter signal abnormalities represent leukoencephalopathy on brain MRI.
Cerebral atrophy is a rare severe brain imaging sequela.

edges.target: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation
Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

edges.description: Elevated HMG reports the blocked leucine-degradation endpoint shared with HMGCL-deficient ketogenesis.
edges.source: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

edges.description: Elevated 3-MGC reports diversion of upstream leucine-catabolism intermediates around the HMGCL block.
edges.source: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

edges.description: Elevated C5-OH/3HIV-C reports leucine-derived acyl-CoA accumulation and is used as the first-tier newborn-screening signal.
edges.source: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

edges.description: Elevated 3-MGL is part of the diagnostic urinary organic acid profile for the leucine-catabolism block.
edges.source: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

edges.description: Inappropriately low ketone bodies during illness report failure of endogenous ketogenesis.
Lower hepatic acetyl-CoA reports the acyl-CoA disequilibrium mechanism that impairs urea-cycle activation in the HMGCL model.
Higher ammonia reports the secondary hyperammonemia branch downstream of impaired acyl-CoA balance.

evidence.reference_title: A newborn screening approach to diagnose 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA lyase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Patients with HMGCLD present with a diagnostic urinary pattern of elevated organic acids such as 3‐hydroxyisovaleric acid (3HIV‐A), 3‐methylglutaconic acid (3MGC‐A), 3‐hydroxy‐3‐methylglutaric acid (3H3MG‐A), 3‐methylglutaric acid (3MG‐A) and in some cases 3‐methylcrotonylglycine.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxy-3-Methylglutaric Acid Disrupts Brain Bioenergetics, Redox Homeostasis, and Mitochondrial Dynamics and Affects Neurodevelopment in Neonatal Wistar Rats.
evidence.snippet: 3-Hydroxy-3-methylglutaric acidemia (HMGA) is a neurometabolic inherited disorder characterized by the predominant accumulation of 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaric acid (HMG) in the brain and biological fluids of patients.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated HMG reports the blocked leucine-degradation endpoint shared with HMGCL-deficient ketogenesis.
readouts.target: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

evidence.reference_title: A newborn screening approach to diagnose 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA lyase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Plasma of these patients contains elevated levels of 3‐hydroxyisovalerylcarnitine (3HIV‐C)

readouts.interpretation: Elevated C5-OH/3HIV-C reports leucine-derived acyl-CoA accumulation and is used as the first-tier newborn-screening signal.
readouts.target: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

evidence.reference_title: A newborn screening approach to diagnose 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA lyase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Using untargeted metabolomic analysis of HMGCLD patient plasma, 3MGC‐A and 3H3MG‐A were found among the most discriminating metabolites between patient and control group.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated 3-MGC reports diversion of upstream leucine-catabolism intermediates around the HMGCL block.
readouts.target: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

evidence.reference_title: A newborn screening approach to diagnose 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA lyase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Patients with HMGCLD present with a diagnostic urinary pattern of elevated organic acids such as 3‐hydroxyisovaleric acid (3HIV‐A), 3‐methylglutaconic acid (3MGC‐A), 3‐hydroxy‐3‐methylglutaric acid (3H3MG‐A), 3‐methylglutaric acid (3MG‐A) and in some cases 3‐methylcrotonylglycine.

readouts.interpretation: Elevated 3-MGL is part of the diagnostic urinary organic acid profile for the leucine-catabolism block.
readouts.target: Leucine catabolic block and organic acid accumulation

nodes.description: Elevated urinary 3-methylglutaric acid is part of the characteristic organic-acid profile downstream of the HMGCL-dependent leucine catabolic block.

evidence.reference_title: 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaric aciduria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0003344 | 3-Methylglutaric aciduria | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: A liver-specific defect of Acyl-CoA degradation produces hyperammonemia, hypoglycemia and a distinct hepatic Acyl-CoA pattern.
evidence.snippet: KIC loading also increased levels of several leucine-related acyl-CoAs and reduced acetyl-CoA levels.

readouts.interpretation: Lower hepatic acetyl-CoA reports the acyl-CoA disequilibrium mechanism that impairs urea-cycle activation in the HMGCL model.

nodes.description: Emergency treatment includes rapid glucose provision (IV dextrose and/or frequent oral carbohydrate), cessation of protein intake, and correction of metabolic acidosis and hyperammonemia. Prompt intervention is essential to prevent death.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme a lyase deficiency: a review.
evidence.snippet: In the first year of life infants with HMG-CoA-LD run a high risk of developing severe hypoglycaemia which can lead to death if prompt intervention does not occur.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of HMG-CoA Lyase Deficiency-Longitudinal Data on Clinical and Nutritional Management of 10 Australian Cases.
evidence.snippet: Dietary management in patients with HMGCL deficiency is well tolerated, and rapid institution of acute supportive metabolic treatment is imperative to optimizing survival and improve outcomes in this disorder.

nodes.description: Fulminant liver failure is an atypical but documented presentation of HMGCLD. One patient in an Australian cohort presented with isolated liver failure.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of HMG-CoA Lyase Deficiency-Longitudinal Data on Clinical and Nutritional Management of 10 Australian Cases.
evidence.snippet: there were two patients that presented atypically-one with fulminant liver failure and the other with isolated developmental delay.

nodes.description: Chronic HMGCL deficiency and acute crises each produce distinct abnormal hepatic acyl-CoA patterns. Leucine metabolite loading increases leucine-related acyl-CoAs while depleting acetyl-CoA. Acetyl-CoA depletion impairs N-acetylglutamate synthesis, which is required for urea cycle activation via carbamoyl phosphate synthetase 1, producing secondary hyperammonemia. This mechanism was confirmed by carglumate rescue of hyperammonemia in a liver-specific HMGCL knockout mouse model.

evidence.reference_title: A liver-specific defect of Acyl-CoA degradation produces hyperammonemia, hypoglycemia and a distinct hepatic Acyl-CoA pattern.
evidence.snippet: Chronic HL deficiency and acute crises each produced distinct abnormal liver acyl-CoA patterns, which would not be predictable from levels of urine organic acids and plasma acylcarnitines.

evidence.reference_title: A liver-specific defect of Acyl-CoA degradation produces hyperammonemia, hypoglycemia and a distinct hepatic Acyl-CoA pattern.
evidence.snippet: KIC-induced hyperammonemia improved following administration of carglumate (N-carbamyl-L-glutamic acid), which substitutes for the product of an acetyl-CoA-dependent reaction essential for urea cycle function

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of HMG-CoA Lyase Deficiency-Longitudinal Data on Clinical and Nutritional Management of 10 Australian Cases.
evidence.snippet: 3-Hydroxy-3-Methylglutaryl-CoA Lyase (HMGCL) deficiency can be a very severe disorder that typically presents with acute metabolic decompensation with features of hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hyperammonemia, and metabolic acidosis.

readouts.interpretation: Higher ammonia reports the secondary hyperammonemia branch downstream of impaired acyl-CoA balance.

nodes.description: Prevention of prolonged fasting is critical because ketogenesis failure means the patient cannot generate alternative energy substrates during fasting. Emergency carbohydrate plans are used during illness.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme a lyase deficiency: a review.
evidence.snippet: The metabolic crisis develops when the infant is first introduced to dietary protein soon after birth, or later, when a reduced intake of glucose, often during a viral infection, results in a drain on the infant's circulating glucose levels.

nodes.description: Cardiomyopathy is a rare but potentially fatal complication of HMGCLD, mechanistically related to the dependence of cardiac tissue on ketone bodies as an energy source during stress.

Carglumic acid (N-carbamyl-L-glutamic acid) is a synthetic analog of N-acetylglutamate that can rescue hyperammonemia by activating carbamoyl phosphate synthetase 1 in the urea cycle, bypassing the acetyl-CoA-dependent NAG synthesis that is impaired in HMGCLD. Demonstrated effective in a mouse model of HMGCLD.

evidence.reference_title: A liver-specific defect of Acyl-CoA degradation produces hyperammonemia, hypoglycemia and a distinct hepatic Acyl-CoA pattern.
evidence.snippet: KIC-induced hyperammonemia improved following administration of carglumate (N-carbamyl-L-glutamic acid), which substitutes for the product of an acetyl-CoA-dependent reaction essential for urea cycle function

nodes.description: Cerebral atrophy was found in 1/62 (1.6%) in a Saudi cohort, representing severe neurological sequelae.

evidence.reference_title: HMG-CoA Lyase Deficiency: A Retrospective Study of 62 Saudi Patients.
evidence.snippet: cerebral atrophy was found in one (1/62; 1.612%) patient.

nodes.description: Restriction of leucine and total protein intake is the cornerstone of long-term management. All patients in contemporary cohorts receive protein-restricted diets, with some also receiving fat restriction. Dietary management is well tolerated and helps prevent metabolic crises.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of HMG-CoA Lyase Deficiency-Longitudinal Data on Clinical and Nutritional Management of 10 Australian Cases.
evidence.snippet: All patients have been on long-term protein restriction, and those diagnosed more recently have had additional fat restriction.

evidence.reference_title: HMG-CoA Lyase Deficiency: A Retrospective Study of 62 Saudi Patients.
evidence.snippet: Our findings suggest that diagnosis at an early stage with careful dietary management may avoid metabolic crises.

nodes.description: Elevated circulating hepatic transaminases are a frequent marker of hepatic involvement during HMG-CoA lyase deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaric aciduria (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002910 | Elevated circulating hepatic transaminase concentration | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Exogenous ketone supplementation (sodium D,L-3-hydroxybutyrate, up to 900 mg/kg/day) bypasses impaired endogenous ketogenesis to provide alternative energy substrates for brain and heart. Used in both acute management and chronic adjunct therapy. Reported as well tolerated and effective in clinical case series.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of HMG-CoA Lyase Deficiency-Longitudinal Data on Clinical and Nutritional Management of 10 Australian Cases.
evidence.snippet: five of nine patients have used 900 mg/kg/day of sodium D,L 3-hydroxybutyrate in combination with intravenous dextrose-containing fluids

nodes.description: Developmental delay occurs in a subset of patients, with 62.6% achieving normal development in a systematic review. Learning disability was reported in 24% and developmental delay in 9.7% of a Saudi cohort.

evidence.reference_title: 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A lyase deficiency: one disease - many faces.
evidence.snippet: The neurologic long-term outcome was favorable with 62.6% of patients showing normal development.

nodes.description: The major accumulating metabolite 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaric acid (HMG) directly disrupts brain mitochondrial function. In neonatal rat brain, HMG exposure reduces succinate dehydrogenase and respiratory chain complex II-III and IV activity in the cerebral cortex and striatum, impairs antioxidant defenses, and increases DRP1 levels indicating mitochondrial fission. These mechanisms connect metabolite accumulation to the neurological vulnerability observed early in life.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxy-3-Methylglutaric Acid Disrupts Brain Bioenergetics, Redox Homeostasis, and Mitochondrial Dynamics and Affects Neurodevelopment in Neonatal Wistar Rats.
evidence.snippet: HMG decreased the activities of succinate dehydrogenase and respiratory chain complexes II-III and IV in the cortex. HMG also decreased the activities of citrate synthase and succinate dehydrogenase, as well as complex IV in the striatum.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxy-3-Methylglutaric Acid Disrupts Brain Bioenergetics, Redox Homeostasis, and Mitochondrial Dynamics and Affects Neurodevelopment in Neonatal Wistar Rats.
evidence.snippet: HMG further increased DRP1 levels in the cortex, indicating mitochondrial fission.

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxy-3-Methylglutaric Acid Disrupts Brain Bioenergetics, Redox Homeostasis, and Mitochondrial Dynamics and Affects Neurodevelopment in Neonatal Wistar Rats.
evidence.snippet: Our findings provide evidence that HMG causes oxidative stress, bioenergetic dysfunction, and neurodevelopmental changes in neonatal rats

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxy-3-Methylglutaric Acid Disrupts Brain Bioenergetics, Redox Homeostasis, and Mitochondrial Dynamics and Affects Neurodevelopment in Neonatal Wistar Rats.
evidence.snippet: HMG-injected animals showed impaired performance in all sensorimotor tests examined.

nodes.description: Biallelic pathogenic variants in HMGCL reduce mitochondrial 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA lyase catalytic activity.

evidence.reference_title: 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A lyase deficiency: one disease - many faces.
evidence.snippet: 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A lyase deficiency (HMGCLD) is an autosomal recessive disorder of ketogenesis and leucine degradation due to mutations in HMGCL.

evidence.reference_title: 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A lyase deficiency: one disease - many faces.
evidence.snippet: This comprehensive data analysis provides a systematic overview on all published cases with HMGCLD including a list of all known HMGCL mutations.

evidence.reference_title: HMG-CoA Lyase Deficiency: A Retrospective Study of 62 Saudi Patients.
evidence.snippet: This is the largest cohort of HMGCLD patients reported from Saudi Arabia, signifying this disorder as a likely life-threatening disease, with a high prevalence in the region.

nodes.description: Hepatomegaly has been reported during acute metabolic crises, reflecting hepatic injury and metabolic stress.
Secondary hyperammonemia occurs during metabolic crises, sometimes exceeding 1000 umol/L. Mechanism involves acetyl-CoA depletion impairing N-acetylglutamate-dependent urea cycle activation.

evidence.reference_title: A liver-specific defect of Acyl-CoA degradation produces hyperammonemia, hypoglycemia and a distinct hepatic Acyl-CoA pattern.
evidence.snippet: Hyperammonemia and hypoglycemia, cardinal features of many inborn errors of acyl-CoA metabolism, occurred spontaneously in some HLLKO mice and were inducible by administering KIC.

nodes.description: Hypoketotic or non-ketotic hypoglycemia is a cardinal feature, resulting from failure of ketogenesis during fasting. Reported in 61% of patients at initial diagnosis in a large cohort.

evidence.reference_title: HMG-CoA Lyase Deficiency: A Retrospective Study of 62 Saudi Patients.
evidence.snippet: 38 patients (61.29%) presented with hypoglycemia

evidence.reference_title: 3-Hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme a lyase deficiency: a review.
evidence.snippet: In the first year of life infants with HMG-CoA-LD run a high risk of developing severe hypoglycaemia which can lead to death if prompt intervention does not occur.

nodes.description: Loss of HMGCL-mediated HMG-CoA cleavage abolishes ketogenesis, depriving brain and heart of essential energy substrates during fasting and catabolic stress. This produces hypoketotic hypoglycemia and metabolic acidosis during acute crises.

evidence.reference_title: 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A lyase deficiency: one disease - many faces.
evidence.snippet: More than 95% of patients presented with acute metabolic decompensation. Most patients manifested within the first year of life, 42.4% already neonatally.

evidence.reference_title: A newborn screening approach to diagnose 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA lyase deficiency.
evidence.snippet: The mitochondrial enzyme is responsible for catalyzing the cleavage of HMG‐CoA to acetyl‐CoA and acetoacetic acid. This conversion is a common last step in leucine catabolism and ketogenesis from fatty acids.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of HMG-CoA Lyase Deficiency-Longitudinal Data on Clinical and Nutritional Management of 10 Australian Cases.
evidence.snippet: 3-Hydroxy-3-Methylglutaryl-CoA Lyase (HMGCL) deficiency can be a very severe disorder that typically presents with acute metabolic decompensation with features of hypoketotic hypoglycemia

readouts.interpretation: Inappropriately low ketone bodies during illness report failure of endogenous ketogenesis.

nodes.description: L-carnitine is commonly prescribed in long-term management to support detoxification and excretion of accumulating organic acids as acylcarnitine conjugates. Used in most patients across contemporary cohorts.

evidence.reference_title: Treatment of HMG-CoA Lyase Deficiency-Longitudinal Data on Clinical and Nutritional Management of 10 Australian Cases.
evidence.snippet: Most patients take L-carnitine.

nodes.description: Reduced arousal and lethargy during decompensation events, often progressing to encephalopathy or coma in severe crises.

HMGCL is also required for leucine degradation. Blockade causes upstream accumulation of leucine-derived metabolites including 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaric acid, 3-methylglutaconic acid, 3-methylglutaric acid, and 3-hydroxyisovaleric acid. Illness-driven leucine flux increases toxic metabolite excretion, implying leucine-derived toxicity is particularly prominent under inflammatory and catabolic stress.

nodes.description: Acute metabolic crises and accumulating organic acids injure vulnerable neural tissue, producing seizures, developmental and speech delay, hypotonia, and MRI abnormalities including leukoencephalopathy and rare cerebral atrophy.

nodes.description: Tandem mass spectrometry screening using elevated C5-OH (3-hydroxyisovalerylcarnitine) enables presymptomatic diagnosis. Confirmation follows with urine organic acid analysis and molecular testing of HMGCL. Early detection allows initiation of dietary management before metabolic decompensation.

evidence.snippet: Our findings suggest that diagnosis at an early stage with careful dietary management may avoid metabolic crises.

nodes.description: Recurrent vomiting during metabolic crises, often the presenting symptom triggering evaluation and emergency management.
A Saudi Arabian founder variant accounting for 77.4% of affected individuals in a 62-patient cohort. This illustrates strong population structure and genotype clustering.
